preFix.title: Slab-built incense holder
preFix.subtitle: A flat clay tray with a drilled hole for stick incense
preFix.excerpt: A flat air-dry clay tray with a drilled holder hole for a single incense stick. The tray catches the ash as it falls. Built from one flat slab, shaped at the edges, and finished with acrylic paint and sealer. No kiln required. Suitable as a desk or shelf accessory for decorative incense use.
preFix.sourceNotes: Incense-holder form derived from first principles. Technique references general flat-slab hand-building practice.

content.text: An incense holder needs two things: a flat surface to catch falling ash, and a hole to hold the stick upright. Both come from a single flat
slab

of air-dry clay. This tutorial produces a rectangular tray approximately 160 mm long and 40 mm wide. No kiln is required. The finished holder is suitable for decorative stick incense only. Do not use for nightlights, tea lights, or any open flame.
Rolling the slab

Set the slab guides on the wooden board. Roll the clay between them to 8 mm thickness. An 8 mm base is thick enough to hold the incense stick without cracking. Cut a rectangle 160 mm long by 40 mm wide using the fettling knife and a ruler.
Shaping the edges

While the clay is still soft, pinch a small raised rim around the long edges of the tray. This rim is about 5 mm high and 3 mm thick and runs along both long sides. It prevents ash from rolling off. Smooth the interior of the rim with a damp fingertip. Leave the two short ends open. Allow the slab to stiffen to
leather-hard
before the next step.
Drilling the holder hole

At the leather-hard stage, use the needle tool to bore a hole through the full thickness of the tray at one end, approximately 20 mm from the short edge and centred on the width. Twist the needle tool slowly while applying gentle downward pressure. The hole should be about 3-4 mm in diameter, enough for a standard incense stick to stand upright. Test the fit with the incense stick before the clay dries fully. If the hole is too tight, widen it gently by rotating the needle tool.
Drying
Leave the tray flat on the board to dry.
Bone-dry

takes 24-48 hours depending on room temperature and humidity. Do not rush the drying; warping is more likely if the tray dries unevenly. If the tray begins to warp, press it flat gently and lay a flat board on top for the final drying hours.
Finishing

Sand any rough edges lightly with fine sandpaper. Paint the tray as desired with acrylic paint. Allow the paint to dry, then apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish as a sealer. The varnish makes the surface wipe-clean for ash residue.
Variations
Press a texture stamp into the flat slab before shaping the rim for a decorated surface.
Drill two or three holes along the tray to hold multiple incense sticks side by side.
Cut the tray into a leaf shape instead of a rectangle for a more organic form.

rows.fix: Place a flat board on top of the tray during the last hours of drying. Drying on plaster or a porous surface also helps by drawing moisture evenly from the base.
rows.cause: Clay dried faster on the top surface than on the underside

rows.fix: Drill at the leather-hard stage only. If cracking has occurred, fill the cracks with fresh slip, smooth, and allow to dry again.
rows.cause: The hole was drilled when the clay was too dry, causing it to fracture rather than compress
rows.problem: Holder hole cracks around the edges

rows.fix: Fill the hole with a coil of fresh clay and re-drill to a tighter diameter when leather-hard again.
rows.cause: Hole is too wide for the stick diameter
rows.problem: Incense stick sits loose in the hole

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
